item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't understand what's so confusing. The article literally states that there is something running around in the pipage..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
theres no need to understand this one, is creepy on its own

maybe having a low res photo of a fucked up drain would help. clog ur own drain for the clout

+1, is still q. good

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have you ever seen roots grow into a pipe? You can't just pull them out and you often have to dismantle or cut the pipes and take the hole thing rather than cut up the roots. I imagine it's a similar situation, and since it's a skip, the Foundation isn't willing to take the chance that cutting it could make the situation worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good stuff. Writing an intentionally short article that actually works is a challenging task, and this one manages to pull it off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think there's a lot in this article I could have gone in detail on, but I decided not to. Horror is every bit as much what isn't shown as what is, and here you have a professional and weathered organisation telling you that whatever this is, it isn't worth removing. And I think that does far more about 5788 than anomalous strength could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The integration of SCP-5788 into the plumbing of the kitchen makes removal and characterisation difficult."

This broke my suspension of disbelief, I found it too implausible that it would be difficult to disconnect the disposal unit from the rest of the plumbing, even if the flesh mass runs all the way through the piping. Unless there is some kind of anomalous force resisting disassembly, of course, but this is not mentioned.

item.title: I dig this
item.content:encoded:
+1

One of the best short articles I've read. Just the right blend of body horror and subtle implications, which gave me a lot to chew on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Saw this in the IRC while we were discussing mystery skips; and this hit on exactly what I meant when I was discussing what a mystery skip needed to work or not, with a fine punch of horror

+1, really good job!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I am just a dumb-dumb who likes vivid imagery and very short SCPs, so this ticks all my boxes, even if I didn't fully grok the implied story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At least it's not just me…
I'm usually pretty good at connecting dots with scips like this, but… Something just isn't clicking for me. Not a bad scip, but I feel I can't appreciate it in its subtlety, so I'll have to no-vote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reimann

:(

But that's exactly what I was aiming for and I'm so happy to see it came across for you! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the kind of thing I love to see in flash fiction. Subtle storytelling that gives you just enough information to make your own conclusions, but still leaves some mystery. Fantastic work Reimann.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I definitely think its too subtle to understand

By that I mean I don't understand it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I cannot disagree more with this sentiment. What makes this piece work is its subtlety and simplicity. It takes a play on words and elevates it past mere punnery and into something both clever and horrifying.

I think that adding something as gratuitously banal as a jumpscare or force-fit some sort of picture into this would be demeaning to the author and the piece itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The SCP fundation will take in everything but the kitchen sink…

…and decided while it would not extract the kitchen sink easily, it could contain the appartment instead, apparently.

https://tvtropes.org/pmwiki/pmwiki.php/Main/KitchenSinkIncluded

as for the SCP itself, I originally planned to upvote it on that alone, but I re-read it and downvoted it. It's TOO SUBTLE TO UNDERSTAND.

You can redeem the SCP by adding an image that gives extra information, and perhaps a jump scare (what if it made sounds on mouseover??).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i have no idea why, but this made me feel intensely sick, which is strange because im an incredibly huge fan of body horror and dont really get sick from it easily. hell, ive read about flesh shit in pipes before and yet, the way this was written just freaked me out a bit.

for that u get a 1+, i miss a good flash fiction article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: <3 pogged out of my mind hearing this from you gee

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is definitely a pogchamp article. Excellent flash fiction with the perfect amount of subtlety.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Votes don't help the author, so I've vowed to always leave a comment with mine, at the very least whenever I rate an article negatively. If you feel the same, help out by leaving critique with more of your votes. Comment follows:

There's ups and downs here.

The biggest down for me is the item in question. I understand that sink disposal thingies are common in the US, and one of my biggest gripes with concepts on the wiki is taking something common in the home and thinking "How can I make this anomalous?". I feel like that's what happened here.

There's some plot here. Enough to make it look like there's connections, but not enough for me to actually draw any. I'm famously poor with reading between the lines so to me the isolated details are just isolated details. It's either not there or it's beyond me, so it's a novote overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Possible answer?
close

They enjoy being together in the pipes?

(If so, spoilers!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kind of?

Spoilers?
Sort of

It's further adventures in minimalist storytelling. There's a marriage, yes, but the two are recently divorced, as hinted by the hotel and name change. The two colours of hair in the sink indicate that both are stuck in there, and the unidentified neural tissue is… not human. I left the sequence of events intentionally vague as to how and why they ended up in the sink, because there's enough hints as to have people form all sorts of theories. And I'm afraid I won't answer the stinger question here though - if they could scream the whole time, why wait until the water?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A stellar exercise in flash fiction. Huge fan of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh, I'm sorry, but I don't really understand what's going on here

Is this some kind of joke about marriage or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gross.

Also, clever.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alternate titles include "Down the Drain", "In Hot Water", "Marriage Disposal", and "Laying Pipe".
Many thanks to weizhong, KaraKatt, A Random Day, cybersqyd, MalyceGraves, Nagiros, and gee0765.

The idea came to me while making some pasta. I wondered how minimalist I could be in telling a story where the only tools I had were screams in a sink and a bit of apartment information.
